episode_title: Ritvvij Parrikh: Personalization Vs. Editorial Judgment? At The Times of India, You Can Have Both
transcript_file: 2024-10-23 - Ritvvij Parrikh Personalization Vs. Editorial Judgment At The Times of India, You Can Have Both.txt
summary_claims: Personalization at Times of India has sustained a CTR increase of almost 85% on the web and 30-40% on the app over the last one and a half years
Around 50% of personalized page views come from stories older than two days, demonstrating the ability to recirculate evergreen archive content

The business of news is all about chasing moving targets -- the editorial team chases the news cycle, distribution teams chase changing SEO algorithms and social media trends, and ad floor prices constantly change
Most off-the-shelf personalization models were not built to run news businesses; news companies using personalization mostly add widgets or tabs rather than running the entire business through it

matches.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: One of the best things about working in a newsroom is that all stories are vetted. All stories are written by journalists. There's no conspiracy theory or there's no fake news out there. So we are in fact operating in a very safe environment. So if there should be a personalization system, it's in a newsroom and not outside one.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: Human editorial judgment is positioned as the irreplaceable foundation that makes newsroom personalization uniquely safe compared to social media platforms. The editorial team provides training data and maintains content moderation guardrails over the algorithm, representing a structured human-in-the-loop model.
matches.matched_text: one of the best things about working in a newsroom is that all stories are vetted. All stories are written by journalists. There's no conspiracy theory or there's no fake news out there. So we are in fact operating in a very safe environment. So if there should be a personalization system, it's in a newsroom and not outside one.

Trust is positioned as a competitive advantage over social media platforms. matches.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: One of the best things about working in a newsroom is that all stories are vetted. All stories are written by journalists.

matches.summary: Ethics are implicit in the editorial guardrails over recommendations (editors can reject inappropriate suggestions, content moderation layer exists) but no formal ethical framework or guidelines are discussed.
matches.matched_text: one of the best things about working in a newsroom is that all stories are vetted. All stories are written by journalists.

matches.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: We use as less data points as possible to personalize. So think of it as a multi-tier system where if you're dipping into the archive, we'll have only one or two attributes associated with each story... So there is constantly this calculation of what is the least amount of information that can give us the highest return.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: This is a core technical episode. Parrikh details the Signals recommender system's multi-tier architecture, the Growth Rx real-time data pipeline requiring banking-level accuracy, the self-learning model without hard-coded rules, and the deliberate choice to build in-house rather than use off-the-shelf solutions. He argues recommender systems should precede LLM investment.
matches.matched_text: we use as less data points as possible to personalize. So think of it as a multi-tier system where if you're dipping into the archive, we'll have only one or two attributes associated with each story. If we then we will go ahead and reduce from let's say the archive, which is probably, I don't know, 10 million stories, bring it down to a million.

matches.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: Generative AI is two, three years old right now. Recommended systems have been around in the market for almost 15 years. We've not fully adopted those and we are imagining that we can participate in the LLM race. It's not practical.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: The industry's failure to adopt 15-year-old recommender system technology before jumping to LLMs is cited as an adoption gap. The cultural shift from editorial-controlled homepages to algorithmic curation required internal buy-in but specific training programs are not discussed.
matches.matched_text: generative AI is two, three years old right now. Recommended systems have been around in the market for almost 15 years. We've not fully adopted those and we are imagining that we can participate in the LLM race. It's not practical.

matches.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: There is no rich country with low energy, like energy as an electricity. Similarly, there is no rich media company that doesn't have strong data pipelines, strong first-party data and deep algorithms.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: Parrikh presents a sophisticated strategic framework for organizational AI adoption: build core capabilities in-house, use hypothesis-driven development over A/B testing, focus on long-term assets rather than one-time projects, and coordinate across revenue models to prevent cannibalization. The house-of-brands structural challenge illustrates how organizational design affects AI strategy.
matches.matched_text: there is no rich country with low energy, like energy as an electricity. Similarly, there is no rich media company that doesn't have strong data pipelines, strong first-party data and deep algorithms.

matches.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: Generative AI is two, three years old right now. Recommended systems have been around in the market for almost 15 years. We've not fully adopted those and we are imagining that we can participate in the LLM race. It's not practical.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: Parrikh highlights the structural inequality between news companies and tech giants -- news is a low average revenue per user product, limiting algorithm investment. Bloomberg is cited as the exception because it is fundamentally a data company. The journalism market is implicitly too small for bespoke AI solutions.
matches.matched_text: generative AI is two, three years old right now. Recommended systems have been around in the market for almost 15 years. We've not fully adopted those and we are imagining that we can participate in the LLM race. It's not practical.

matches.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: I foresee that in the coming years, recommender systems and news businesses will evolve to operate more like fintech slash mutual fund algorithms where we look at each revenue model -- affiliate, subscription, sponsored content, direct ads, indirect ads -- as a different asset class similar to equity, debt, gold, real estate.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: Personalization is the episode's dominant product theme. The Signals system achieved 85% CTR increase on web, recirculates 50% of page views from stories older than two days, and operates as a self-learning system. Parrikh envisions news personalization evolving toward fintech-style multi-asset optimization across revenue models.

So think of it as a multi-tier system where if you're dipping into the archive, we'll have only one or two attributes associated with each story. If we then we will go ahead and reduce from let's say the archive, which is probably, I don't know, 10 million stories, bring it down to a million. add few more data attributes. Then from a million down to maybe 100k, add few more data attributes.

matches.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: There is no rich country with low energy, like energy as an electricity. Similarly, there is no rich media company that doesn't have strong data pipelines, strong first-party data and deep algorithms.

matches.summary: Business model innovation is central. Parrikh describes multiple revenue model cannibalization as a structural problem, proposes algorithmic revenue optimization across asset classes, and positions first-party data and proprietary algorithms as existential competitive advantages. The archive recirculation (50% of personalized views from older stories) demonstrates new revenue extraction from existing content.
matches.matched_text: there is no rich country with low energy, like energy as an electricity. Similarly, there is no rich media company that doesn't have strong data pipelines, strong first-party data and deep algorithms.

matches.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: We use as less data points as possible to personalize. So think of it as a multi-tier system where if you're dipping into the archive, we'll have only one or two attributes associated with each story.
best_quote.speaker: Ritvvij Parrikh

matches.summary: The episode describes a mature production system rather than an experimental pipeline. Scaling challenges are addressed through the multi-tier data architecture and the challenge of maintaining data accuracy amid concept drift. The critique of A/B testing in news (conditions change, making validated tests invalid) is a methodological insight about the production gap.
matches.matched_text: we use as less data points as possible to personalize. So think of it as a multi-tier system where if you're dipping into the archive, we'll have only one or two attributes associated with each story.

matches.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: We use as less data points as possible to personalize. So there is constantly this calculation of what is the least amount of information that can give us the highest return.

matches.summary: Data minimization is a deliberate design principle -- using the fewest data points possible for maximum return. Data quality is treated with banking-level rigor. First-party data collection and user login incentives are strategic priorities, though privacy-specific concerns (source protection, data governance) are not explicitly discussed.
